findings.claim: The `@mermaid-js/parser@0.6.3` package does NOT export a `flowchart` parser ā€” the exit-criterion test is structurally impossible to pass with this dependency choice.
findings.detail: Inspection of the installed parser's type declarations at node_modules/@mermaid-js/parser/dist/src/parse.d.ts shows the supported overloads are: 'info' | 'packet' | 'pie' | 'architecture' | 'gitGraph' | 'radar' | 'treemap'. There is no 'flowchart' overload. Calling `parseMermaid('flowchart', mermaid)` will either throw `MermaidParseError` for an unrecognized grammar or fail type-check at strict mode. The scaffolder template copies this same broken assertion into every generated project (Gate 5 in smoke.test.ts) ā€” so the headline 10-minute DX path CANNOT pass out of the box for any new developer. This is the single largest DX regression in G8.
findings.suggested_fix: Two options, ordered by preference: (a) Replace the `@mermaid-js/parser` dependency with the full `mermaid` CLI or a standalone flowchart parser, and exercise the diagram through Mermaid's own `parse()` in `mermaid@^11`. (b) Drop the parser assertion and replace with a narrow structural check: verify the output matches a hand-written flowchart grammar regex (starts with `flowchart TD`, every `-->` references a declared node id, every node id conforms to `^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$`). Option (b) is cheaper and avoids pulling the full mermaid runtime into every scaffolded project. Whichever path: update BOTH the G8-B internal test AND the scaffolded template's smoke test so the scaffolder exit criterion is coherent with the internal tests.

findings.claim: ESM/CJS mismatch: the napi package declares `"type": "module"` but its generated `index.js` is CommonJS ā€” this forces every consumer (including the scaffolded project) into the `createRequire` fallback path the DSL wrapper uses, and any non-Node bundler will hit the same friction.
findings.detail: `bindings/napi/index.js` (the napi-rs-generated dispatcher) opens with `const { readFileSync } = require('node:fs')` and ends with `module.exports = nativeBinding`. That is CJS. But `bindings/napi/package.json` sets `"type": "module"`, which tells Node to load `.js` files as ESM. Result: a bare `import '@benten/engine-native'` throws `ReferenceError: require is not defined`. packages/engine/src/engine.ts papers over this with a hand-rolled `createRequire` + per-triplet filename fallback (engine.ts:98-130) ā€” the comment admits `G8-A's bindings/napi/package.json declares "type": "module", which causes the napi-rs-generated CJS index.js to fail under ESM loading`. This works inside packages/engine but a scaffolded project that imports `@benten/engine-native` directly (even via a future tool) would break. The workaround is load-bearing and untested by scaffolder CI.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `"type": "module"` from `bindings/napi/package.json`, OR rename the dispatcher to `index.cjs` and update the `main` field, OR use the napi-rs v3 `--esm` build flag to emit an ESM dispatcher. The cleanest fix: drop `"type": "module"` and keep `main: ./index.js`. Delete the fragile triplet-fallback block in engine.ts:98-130 (80 lines of code) once the mismatch is resolved. Add a test in bindings/napi/index.test.ts that does a bare `import { Engine } from '@benten/engine-native'` under Node --experimental-vm-modules to prove the loading works both ways.

findings.claim: `engine.trace()` returns a result the DSL FABRICATES by running a second non-traced call; per-step inputs/outputs are NEVER populated; durationUs is clamped to `max(1, native)` to hide integer-zero timings.
findings.detail: The native binding at bindings/napi/src/trace.rs emits ONLY `{ nodeCid, durationUs }` per step ā€” no `primitive`, no `inputs`, no `outputs`, no `error`. The TS types at packages/engine/src/types.ts:95-108 advertise all four extra fields as the contract. The wrapper at engine.ts:528-543 fills `primitive` with an empty string (`String(s.primitive ?? '')`), leaves `inputs`/`outputs`/`error` undefined, and ā€” worse ā€” silently runs a SECOND non-traced call inside `trace()` (engine.ts:514-525) to synthesize `Trace.result` because the native binding doesn't return it. A developer debugging a failing handler sees: (i) fabricated timings that can't be trusted (the `max(1, x)` clamp), (ii) empty primitive names, (iii) no I/O snapshots at each step, (iv) a `result` field from an entirely different handler invocation (different Node CID, different createdAt stamp). This is not a useful debugging tool ā€” it's a liability. 'engine.trace' is one of three headline DX deliverables (QUICKSTART.md:86-88); this fails the contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend bindings/napi/src/trace.rs to emit `primitive`, `inputs`, `outputs`, `error`, and `result`. If `benten_engine::Trace` doesn't yet carry those fields, extend it ā€” this is the whole point of a tracer. Remove the synthetic second-call path in engine.ts:514-525 (it's observable: two Nodes materialize per `trace()` call, cluttering storage). Remove the `max(1, durationUs)` clamp ā€” a genuine zero-microsecond step means the tracer lost the timing, and that deserves a clean 0 rather than a lie. Update types.ts to make `primitive` non-optional (it should always be present post-fix).

findings.claim: The zero-config `crud('post')` exit-criterion demo ā€” the headline value proposition of Phase 1 ā€” is documented as failing because native IVM receives identity-only ChangeEvents.
findings.detail: Per the G8 dispatch brief: 'G8-B's crud_post_zero_config_full_cycle test fails because native IVM receives identity-only ChangeEvents (no Node payload)'. This is the demo a new developer runs first: scaffold ā†’ create three posts ā†’ list them. If `engine.call(handler.id, 'post:list', {})` returns an empty or wrongly-ordered list because IVM can't populate view 3 without the Node payload, the developer's first experience is a broken list. QUICKSTART.md step 3 (line 42-48) is exactly this pattern; smoke.test.ts Gate 2 depends on it. The test's `listed.items` assertion requires the list to contain three items with titles ā€” the broken IVM path makes this unreliable. This is the MOST VISIBLE DX GAP. A new dev doesn't care about R5 pipeline state; they care that `npm test` passes on a fresh scaffold.
findings.suggested_fix: G5 IVM integration: extend the ChangeEvent that benten-graph emits to carry the written Node's full payload (labels + properties) so benten-ivm's list-view (View 3) can accumulate rows. Alternatively, have the list-view read from graph storage using the CID from the ChangeEvent ā€” adds a read per write but recovers the invariant that `crud(list)` reflects all prior `crud(create)`s. This is a G5 fix, but G8's smoke test cannot pass without it, so G8 should be marked as blocked on G5 rather than nominally complete. Block the R5-gate advance until the full cycle test passes green end-to-end.

findings.claim: `Engine.open` is thinly async ā€” its only `await` is on native loading ā€” but every call-site pays the async ceremony. More importantly, `Engine.close()` is documented as relying on GC (engine.ts:240-245) which means a developer who calls `engine.close()` and then re-opens the same redb file may hit a lock contention error because napi-rs drops the handle on GC, not deterministically.
findings.detail: Comment at engine.ts:243-245: 'Phase 1 delegates to GC (napi drops the handle when the wrapper is unreferenced); later phases may wire an explicit close method on the native class.' This is fine for long-lived single-process use, but the scaffolded template at template/src/index.ts:14 opens against `.benten/{name}.redb`, writes, and exits ā€” if a developer runs `npm run dev` twice back-to-back and Node hasn't GC'd between invocations (separate processes so it will), OR if they embed it in a test that opens/closes repeatedly in one process (which is what Vitest `beforeAll`/`afterAll` does across the test files), they can hit `file locked` errors from redb. Vitest can and does reuse a worker for sequential test files. Run the three test files sequentially in one worker and the third will deadlock on the redb lock.
findings.suggested_fix: Expose `Engine::close(&mut self)` on the napi binding that calls the Rust-side `engine.close()`/`drop`. Have the TS `close()` method `this.inner.close()` deterministically. Smoke-test harness: open ā†’ close ā†’ open ā†’ close the SAME file in one process and assert no error. Add to crud.test.ts or a new engine-lifecycle.test.ts.

findings.claim: The TS wrapper carries 75 lines of client-side compensation logic for missing native surface behavior: createdAt stamping, createdAt re-attach on GET, `list`ā†’`items` shape translation, `properties` flattening. Each is papering over a napi-layer gap and will drift from engine semantics as Phase 2 extends the evaluator.
findings.detail: Lines 400-416 inject `createdAt` into the input before it hits Rust; lines 429-431 backfill `createdAt` on the response when Rust doesn't echo it; lines 432-434 stash `(handlerId, cid) -> createdAt` in a local Map; lines 437-446 re-attach the stashed value on GET; lines 448-468 rename `list` ā†’ `items` and unwrap `{labels, properties}` ā†’ `properties`. Every one of these is a sign that the wire contract between TS and Rust wasn't fully fleshed out for the CRUD demo ā€” the TS side is lying about what the engine returns. Three risks: (a) a developer debugging with `trace` (which calls the native path directly) sees different shapes than `call` (which goes through this translation), (b) any bug reproduced outside `@benten/engine` won't reproduce the same way as inside, (c) Phase 2 features that extend CRUD (e.g. capability-gated reads) must pass through this same translation layer or break silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Move createdAt injection into the Rust `register_crud` subgraph ā€” the WRITE Node in the crud subgraph should carry a `stamp: true` flag that tells the evaluator to call the HLC at write time. Move the `list` response shaping into the Rust side of the canonical CRUD handler so `list` returns `{ items: [...] }` natively. Flatten `{labels, properties}` ā†’ properties in the native `call` result for CRUD handlers. Then delete engine.ts:395-469 (strip to ~10 lines). The result is a single source of truth for the CRUD wire shape and a TS wrapper that does only type conversion, not semantics.

findings.claim: Two module-level mutable globals (`__nodeCounter`, `__lastHlc`) make the DSL non-thread-safe and break the 'handler CID is stable across invocations' guarantee in concurrent test runs.
findings.detail: `__nodeCounter` at dsl.ts:128 is a module-level `let` incremented by every `addNode` across every SubgraphBuilder and CaseBuilder in the entire process. Two parallel Vitest workers (which is Vitest's default) building a `crud('post')` subgraph will produce different node ids (`read-1` vs `read-101` depending on test scheduling), meaning the structural subgraph differs run-to-run, meaning the handler CID the engine computes differs run-to-run, meaning cross-process tests that snapshot the CID will flake. The comment at dsl.ts:535-538 on `crud()` explicitly claims 'Re-invoking crud('post') in a separate call site yields a structurally-identical subgraph so handler CIDs are stable across invocations' ā€” that claim is false under the current counter scheme. Same issue with `__lastHlc` at dsl.ts:485.
findings.suggested_fix: Make node ids deterministic per-builder: reset `__nodeCounter` to 0 at SubgraphBuilder construction (`this.counter = 0`) and count per-instance. The DSL test suite should also assert stable CID across two independent `crud('post')` calls in the same process ā€” this catches the regression. `__lastHlc` is a deliberate monotonic HLC source and its per-process nature is acceptable ā€” but document the contract: `crud()` handlers receiving a custom `hlc` in `CrudOptions` is the deterministic-test escape hatch, and tests SHOULD use it.

findings.claim: The scaffolded project's package.json lists `@benten/engine: "*"` as a dependency ā€” but inside a fresh checkout, `npm install` against a generated project outside the workspace will fail to find `@benten/engine` on any registry.
findings.detail: The bin.mjs comment (line 17-19) notes 'the generated project points at @benten/engine via the workspace link configured at the repo root package.json. Inside a checked-out Benten repo this lets the scaffolder test harness install-and-test without pushing to a registry.' Correct ā€” but the test harness at tools/create-benten-app/test/scaffolder.test.ts:36 runs `npm install --silent --no-audit --no-fund` inside a TEMPORARY directory under os.tmpdir(). That dir is NOT inside the workspace tree, so npm has no workspace link to resolve `@benten/engine: "*"` against the real `packages/engine/`. The install WILL fail (registry has no `@benten/engine`). The scaffolder_produces_working_project test will fail for that reason, independent of any other G8 issue.
findings.suggested_fix: Two approaches: (a) The scaffolder rewrites `package.json`'s dependency to `file:<absolute-path-to-repo>/packages/engine` when scaffolding inside a dev checkout ā€” detect the dev mode via the presence of a `.benten-dev-workspace` marker file at the repo root, or by examining the scaffolder's own package.json's `@benten/engine` field. (b) Pre-publish both packages locally via `npm pack` and install from the tarball. Approach (a) is cleaner. Update scaffolder.test.ts to verify the rewritten dependency points at a real path before running `npm install`, so the failure mode is 'bad rewrite' not 'bad registry'.

findings.location: packages/engine/src/dsl.ts:174-199 (all primitive helpers type args loosely)
findings.claim: The DSL primitive helpers accept primitive-specific arg interfaces but cast to `Record<string, JsonValue>` via `as Record<string, JsonValue>` ā€” eight call sites. This silently drops type errors for caller-side typos like `read({ lable: 'post' })` (typo) because the cast widens the type before the structural check fires.
findings.detail: `read(args: ReadArgs)` accepts a typed ReadArgs, then casts `{ ...args } as Record<string, JsonValue>` to stuff it into a generic bag. The cast succeeds even when `args` shape is wrong ā€” for example, accidentally passing a non-JsonValue (a Date, a function) would be masked until runtime. More importantly, a typo on a field name (`lable` not `label`) is caught at the outer function call, but only if `ReadArgs` is a `strict` shape ā€” which it is declared as, so this one is OK. The actual risk: downstream, the engine wrapper reads `n.args.label` and trusts it; if TransformArgs is extended in Phase 2 to add an optional field and the generic Record<string, JsonValue> bag allows excess properties, the downstream typecheck goes stale.
findings.suggested_fix: Use satisfies instead of a cast: `args satisfies Record<string, JsonValue>`. For primitive args, store the typed record directly on the SubgraphNode as a discriminated union: `type SubgraphNode = { primitive: 'read', args: ReadArgs, ... } | { primitive: 'write', args: WriteArgs, ... } | ...`. This lets mermaid.ts and engine.ts do exhaustive-case matching on `node.primitive` and access the right args type without a cast. Slight refactor; high ROI for DX safety.

findings.claim: When a native error carries no catalog code, mapNativeError falls back to a synthetic `E_UNKNOWN` code ā€” but the `CODE_RX` regex at errors.ts:149 is tolerant enough to match any E-prefixed token in the message, leading to false-positive 'typed' wraps.
findings.detail: The regex `\bE_[A-Z0-9_]+\b` will match any token that starts with `E_` in any position in the message string. If a native error message happens to contain `E_XXX` substrings in user data (a capability name like `E_CAP_DENIED_FOR_RESOURCE`, or a substring pulled from user input), it will be misclassified. Worse, the fallback at errors.ts:196-199 assembles `E_UNKNOWN` at runtime via `['E', 'UNKNOWN'].join('_')` to dodge the drift detector ā€” which is a code smell: the drift detector is the right place to express 'this code is synthetic and not catalog-backed'; it should be added to an allowlist, not evaded.
findings.suggested_fix: (a) Anchor the regex to require the code appear at a known location ā€” native errors should prefix: `ERROR [E_XXX]: message`. Match only `^ERROR \[(E_[A-Z0-9_]+)\]` or similar stable shape. (b) If a match is attempted and the captured code isn't in CATALOG_CODES, return the fallback BentenError UNCONDITIONALLY (don't try to typed-wrap) ā€” a code not in the catalog means we don't have a fix hint. (c) Allowlist `E_UNKNOWN` in the drift detector rather than runtime-assembling the string to evade it.

findings.claim: flattenCallResult conditionally unwraps `r.result` only when `r.ok !== false` ā€” but the TS types claim the wrapper returns a non-null `Record<string, JsonValue>`. If the native side returns `{ ok: false, error: {...} }`, the function silently returns that raw shape as if it were a success, and the caller sees `result.cid === undefined` without any thrown error.
findings.detail: Lines 609-626: If `r.ok === false`, the early-unwrap path is skipped, and the raw `r` object is returned. This means a 'failed' native call surfaces to the user as a successful-looking object with `ok: false, error: {...}` fields. No throw, no error code, no typed wrapper. Silent failure. Contrast with the thoughtful `mapNativeError` path ā€” that only fires if the native surface throws. If the native surface returns `{ ok: false }` instead of throwing, the wrapper doesn't notice.
findings.suggested_fix: At the top of flattenCallResult: if `r.ok === false`, throw `mapNativeError(r.error ?? r)` so the typed-wrapper contract fires. Audit the native binding ā€” does `Engine::call` return `{ ok: false }` anywhere, or does it always throw? If always throws, delete the `r.ok !== false` guard entirely (it's defensive against a non-existent case). Add a test that asserts a capability denial either throws OR returns a shape that doesn't look like success.

findings.claim: Document still carries the 'Status: Planning' banner from pre-Phase-1, even though Phase 1 now ships the scaffolder + DSL + error catalog. A new developer reading QUICKSTART.md sees 'treat it as a specification of intent' and will assume nothing works.
findings.detail: Line 3: 'Status: Planning (engine code lands in Phase 1). This document specifies what the 10-minute developer experience will look like.' Lines 97-106 enumerate deliverables as unchecked (`[ ]`). The scaffolder, crud, toMermaid, engine.trace are all Phase 1 deliverables and are landed in G8. The document needs a status update that matches reality. A dev who finds QUICKSTART from Google won't know the file is stale.
findings.suggested_fix: Update line 3 to 'Status: Phase 1 complete ā€” see [changelog] for caveats'. Check the boxes in 97-104 that G8 actually delivers; leave dev-server-with-hot-reload unchecked (correctly deferred to Phase 2). Add a 'Known issues' section naming the two critical gaps: (a) `trace()` inputs/outputs are stubs, full fidelity in Phase 2, (b) `list` requires a specific ChangeEvent+IVM integration; works once G5 lands.

findings.claim: 'Did you mean?' matcher for unregistered handler names uses substring + 3-gram, no edit distance. For `handlerId = 'post'` (typo for `crud:post`), the substring match finds it; for `handlerId = 'poost'` (classic typo), the 3-gram fallback emits only the 3-grams `poo`, `oos`, `ost` ā€” `crud:post` contains `ost` and `pos` but not `poo` or `oos`, so only `ost` matches. Works for this one. For `handerId = 'cruud:post'`? None of `cru`, `ruu`, `uud`, `ud:`, `d:p`, `:po`, `pos`, `ost` overlap badly with `crud:post` in a signal way, so it'd hit ā€” OK. But `handlerId = 'cryd:post'` would miss: `cry`, `ryd`, `yd:`, etc. match none.
findings.detail: Narrow edge case; the feature mostly works. But a 2-char typo early in the string fails silently. Developer sees 'no handler cryd:post registered; no near matches' and wastes time.
findings.suggested_fix: Swap to a minimum-edit-distance-of-2 filter alongside the substring/3-gram. leven (or a 20-line fast-path bounded-Levenshtein) catches the common one-char-typo case. Keep the result limit at 3.

findings.claim: `npm run dev` runs the template's index.ts ONCE ā€” registers the handler, logs, exits. That's not a 'dev script' in the modern sense; a developer expecting a watching dev loop will be confused.
findings.detail: The scaffolded project's README at template/README.md:7-12 describes `npm run dev` as 'registers the handler against a local .benten/{name}.redb'. The underlying script at package.json:8 is `node --import tsx src/index.ts` ā€” opens engine, registers, prints, closes, exits. No watch, no HMR, no dev server. QUICKSTART.md:98-104 honestly labels 'Dev server with hot reload' as Phase 2; the scaffolder inherits that honesty by NOT shipping one. But the current script is closer to `node -e 'openAndClose()'` than to `npm run dev` in any other TS project. A developer's mental model (from Next.js, Vite, etc.) will expect a server. The mismatch costs 2 minutes of 'why does it exit?'
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) Rename to `npm run verify` or `npm run register` which honestly describes what it does, and leave `npm run dev` out of the template. (b) Add a trivial `while (true) { await sleep(1000); if (sigint) break; }` loop that keeps the engine open and prints a 'ready' line, so the developer can kick test clients at it. (b) is better because it matches expectations and the open-handle is actually useful. Label it clearly: 'runs the handler against a local DB and waits'.

findings.claim: Template's tsconfig uses `moduleResolution: bundler` ā€” which is correct for modern tooling, but the scaffolded project's `npm run build` calls `tsc` (not a bundler), and tsc with moduleResolution:bundler gives non-standard emit. A dev running `npm run build` then `node dist/index.js` hits 'Cannot find module @benten/engine'.
findings.detail: tsconfig.json:5 sets `moduleResolution: bundler`. The scaffolded package.json:9 runs plain `tsc` to emit dist/. tsc with `moduleResolution: bundler` emits imports without .js extensions on relative imports ā€” which Node ESM rejects at runtime. `node dist/index.js` will throw ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND on the `./handlers.js` import (or `./handlers` without extension, depending on how tsx rewrote it). The template's own `npm test` doesn't hit this because vitest resolves TS sources directly; the break only shows up when a dev follows the README's 'npm run build' step.
findings.suggested_fix: Change `moduleResolution` to `nodenext` (or `node16`) for the tsconfig target, OR switch the build script to `tsup src/index.ts --format esm --dts` (pulls in a bundler dep but produces a clean output). Simplest: use `nodenext` and require `.js` extensions on relative imports in the template source (the index.ts already does this: `from './handlers.js'`), confirming the import paths already target ESM-node semantics.

findings.claim: BranchBuilder exposes both `endBranch()` and `build()` but the common method chaining pattern does neither ā€” a developer writing `crud(...)` imperatively expects `.branch({...}).case('a', s=>s.respond()).case('b', s=>s.respond()).build()` to work, and that DOES work (build is on BranchBuilder), BUT chaining any non-case method after the last `.case()` silently drops subsequent ops because the user is still holding a BranchBuilder, not a SubgraphBuilder.
findings.detail: `s.branch({on: 'x'}).case('a', ...).case('b', ...).transform({...})` ā€” compile error, because transform isn't on BranchBuilder. Dev sees a type error and is confused: 'where do I put the merge/commit?' The fluent API doesn't have an obvious 'I'm done with this branch, resume the outer subgraph' notation. `endBranch()` exists but isn't documented in the README; the user must discover it from IntelliSense.
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) Rename `endBranch` to something more natural like `merge()` or `endCase()`, and document it in the README + dsl.ts JSDoc. (b) Auto-merge on any non-case method call: have BranchBuilder proxy to parent for methods it doesn't own, threading them through. (a) is simpler. A one-line README mention suffices. Also add a compile-time test that exercises a multi-step chain after a branch.

findings.claim: The CATALOG_CODES array is generated as `as const` ā€” good for literal typing ā€” but every time a code is added the downstream `CODE_TO_CTOR` map at errors.ts:105-141 must be hand-updated, or the new code is never routed to its typed class.
findings.detail: errors.ts:105 is a manually-maintained Record<string, BentenErrorCtor> mapping codes to constructors. errors.generated.ts is auto-generated from the catalog. If a new code lands in ERROR-CATALOG.md, codegen picks it up and generates a new error class, but errors.ts doesn't know about it until a human edits the map. The drift detector at scripts/drift-detect-error-catalog.mjs would need to catch this; is it checked? The file path suggests yes, but the risk is nonzero.
findings.suggested_fix: Generate `CODE_TO_CTOR` as part of errors.generated.ts too ā€” it's pure mechanical output from the catalog (for each code X, `[X]: EX` where EX is the class name). Then errors.ts just re-exports the generated map alongside mapNativeError. Eliminates the drift hazard entirely. Trivial diff in scripts/codegen-errors.ts.

most_critical_dx_gap: g8-dx-1 ā€” the @mermaid-js/parser dependency does not ship a 'flowchart' parser. Exit-criterion Gate 5 in the SCAFFOLDED TEMPLATE is impossible to pass. A new developer following QUICKSTART.md hits `npm test` and sees one of the six headline assertions fail with a parser-not-found or unrecognized-diagram error. The test is encoded into the very template that scaffolds every new project, so EVERY developer runs into this on their first command. This is the thing a new developer would hit first, and it invalidates the entire exit-criterion claim.
recommended_gate_decision: BLOCK R5-gate advance until at least the three most critical issues are resolved: g8-dx-1 (mermaid parser), g8-dx-2 (ESM/CJS mismatch + triplet fallback), g8-dx-4 (IVM list-view integration). g8-dx-3 (trace fidelity) can slip to a Phase 2 milestone IF the Phase 1 docs honestly describe the stub state ā€” but the current types/docs claim full fidelity, which needs correction even for partial delivery.
notes: Overall architecture of G8 is sound ā€” the split into a pure TS DSL (no native dependency at import time), a codegen'd error catalog, and a scaffolder that produces a workspace-linked project is exactly right for Phase 1 DX. The 75 lines of TS compensation logic in engine.ts (CRUD flattening, createdAt stamping, trace result fabrication) should NOT live in the wrapper long-term ā€” they're a sign G8 was trying to meet test contracts without coordinated fixes to the native surface (G8-A) and the IVM integration (G5). Each is a small, clear fix; collectively they mean the Phase 1 'the test IS the spec' contract was met by the TS side moving toward the spec rather than the Rust side meeting it. Reconcile the wire contract before declaring G8 done.
